Understanding Go Concurrency Patterns

Published: January 15, 2024 | Author: Jane Developer

Introduction

Go's concurrency model, built around goroutines and channels, provides an elegant way to write concurrent programs. Unlike thread-based models, Go's approach is lightweight and composable.

Goroutines: Lightweight Threads

Goroutines are functions that run concurrently with other functions. They're extremely lightweight—you can spawn thousands without significant overhead.

go func() {
    fmt.Println("Running concurrently!")
}()

Channels: Communication Primitives

Channels provide a way for goroutines to communicate safely. They're typed conduits through which you can send and receive values.

ch := make(chan int)
go func() { ch <- 42 }()
value := <-ch // receives 42

Buffered vs Unbuffered

Unbuffered channels block until both sender and receiver are ready. Buffered channels allow sends to proceed without a receiver, up to the buffer size.

Select Statement

The select statement lets a goroutine wait on multiple channel operations. It's like switch but for channels.

select {
case msg := <-ch1:
    fmt.Println("Received from ch1:", msg)
case ch2 <- value:
    fmt.Println("Sent to ch2")
default:
    fmt.Println("No communication ready")
}

Common Patterns

Worker Pool

A worker pool distributes work across a fixed number of goroutines. This is useful for limiting concurrency and resource usage.

Fan-Out/Fan-In

Fan-out distributes work to multiple goroutines. Fan-in collects results from multiple sources into a single channel.

Conclusion

Go's concurrency primitives are simple yet powerful. By understanding goroutines, channels, and select, you can build efficient concurrent systems.
